# ClusterCockpit Metric Store

[![Build & Test](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-store/actions/workflows/test.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-store/actions/workflows/test.yml)

The cc-metric-store provides a simple in-memory time series database for storing
metrics of cluster nodes at preconfigured intervals. It is meant to be used as
part of the [ClusterCockpit suite](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit). As all
data is kept in-memory (but written to disk as compressed JSON for long term
storage), accessing it is very fast. It also provides topology aware
aggregations over time _and_ nodes/sockets/cpus.

There are major limitations: Data only gets written to disk at periodic
checkpoints, not as soon as it is received. Also only the fixed configured
duration is stored and available.

Go look at the [GitHub
Issues](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-store/issues) for a progress
overview. The [NATS.io](https://nats.io/) based writing endpoint consumes messages in [this
format of the InfluxDB line
protocol](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-specifications/blob/master/metrics/lineprotocol_alternative.md).

## Building

`cc-metric-store` can be built using the provided `Makefile`.
It supports the following targets:

- `make`: Build the application, copy a example configuration file and generate
  checkpoint folders if required.
- `make clean`: Clean the golang build cache and application binary
- `make distclean`: In addition to the clean target also remove the `./var`
  folder
- `make swagger`: Regenerate the Swagger files from the source comments.
- `make test`: Run test and basic checks.

## REST API Endpoints

The REST API is documented in [swagger.json](./api/swagger.json). You can
explore and try the REST API using the integrated [SwaggerUI web
interface](http://localhost:8082/swagger).

For more information on the `cc-metric-store` REST API have a look at the
ClusterCockpit documentation [website](https://clustercockpit.org/docs/reference/cc-metric-store/ccms-rest-api/)

## Run tests

Some benchmarks concurrently access the `MemoryStore`, so enabling the
[Race Detector](https://golang.org/doc/articles/race_detector) might be useful.
The benchmarks also work as tests as they do check if the returned values are as
expected.

```sh
# Tests only
go test -v ./...

# Benchmarks as well
go test -bench=. -race -v ./...
```

## What are these selectors mentioned in the code?

The cc-metric-store works as a time-series database and uses the InfluxDB line
protocol as input format. Unlike InfluxDB, the data is indexed by one single
strictly hierarchical tree structure. A selector is build out of the tags in the
InfluxDB line protocol, and can be used to select a node (not in the sense of a
compute node, can also be a socket, cpu, ...) in that tree. The implementation
calls those nodes `level` to avoid confusion. It is impossible to access data
only by knowing the _socket_ or _cpu_ tag, all higher up levels have to be
specified as well.

This is what the hierarchy currently looks like:

- cluster1
  - host1
    - socket0
    - socket1
    - ...
    - cpu1
    - cpu2
    - cpu3
    - cpu4
    - ...
    - gpu1
    - gpu2
  - host2
  - ...
- cluster2
- ...

Example selectors:

1. `["cluster1", "host1", "cpu0"]`: Select only the cpu0 of host1 in cluster1
2. `["cluster1", "host1", ["cpu4", "cpu5", "cpu6", "cpu7"]]`: Select only CPUs 4-7 of host1 in cluster1
3. `["cluster1", "host1"]`: Select the complete node. If querying for a CPU-specific metric such as floats, all CPUs are implied

## Config file

You find the configuration options on the ClusterCockpit [website](https://clustercockpit.org/docs/reference/cc-metric-store/ccms-configuration/).

## Test the complete setup (excluding cc-backend itself)

There are two ways for sending data to the cc-metric-store, both of which are
supported by the
[cc-metric-collector](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-collector).
This example uses NATS, the alternative is to use HTTP.

```sh
# Only needed once, downloads the docker image
docker pull nats:latest

# Start the NATS server
docker run -p 4222:4222 -ti nats:latest
```

Second, build and start the
[cc-metric-collector](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-collector)
using the following as Sink-Config:

```json
{
  "type": "nats",
  "host": "localhost",
  "port": "4222",
  "database": "updates"
}
```

Third, build and start the metric store. For this example here, the
`config.json` file already in the repository should work just fine.

```sh
# Assuming you have a clone of this repo in ./cc-metric-store:
cd cc-metric-store
make
./cc-metric-store
```

And finally, use the API to fetch some data. The API is protected by JWT based
authentication if `jwt-public-key` is set in `config.json`. You can use this JWT
for testing:
`REDACTED`

```sh
JWT="REDACTED"

# If the collector and store and nats-server have been running for at least 60 seconds on the same host, you may run:
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JWT" -D - "http://localhost:8080/api/query" -d "{ \"cluster\": \"testcluster\", \"from\": $(expr $(date +%s) - 60), \"to\": $(date +%s), \"queries\": [{
  \"metric\": \"load_one\",
  \"host\": \"$(hostname)\"
}] }"

# ...
```

For debugging there is a debug endpoint to dump the current content to stdout:

```sh
JWT="REDACTED"

# If the collector and store and nats-server have been running for at least 60 seconds on the same host, you may run:
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JWT" -D - "http://localhost:8080/api/debug"

# ...
```

1→// Copyright (C) NHR@FAU, University Erlangen-Nuremberg.
     2→// All rights reserved. This file is part of cc-metric-store.
     3→// Use of this source code is governed by a MIT-style
     4→// license that can be found in the LICENSE file.
     5→
     6→package main
     7→
     8→import (
     9→	"context"
    10→	"flag"
    11→	"fmt"
    12→	"os"
    13→	"os/signal"
    14→	"runtime/debug"
    15→	"sync"
    16→	"syscall"
    17→
    18→	"github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-backend/pkg/metricstore"
    19→	ccconf "github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-lib/v2/ccConfig"
    20→	cclog "github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-lib/v2/ccLogger"
    21→	"github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-lib/v2/nats"
    22→	"github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-lib/v2/runtime"
    23→	"github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-store/internal/api"
    24→	"github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-store/internal/config"
    25→	"github.com/google/gops/agent"
    26→)
    27→
    28→const (
    29→	envGOGC = "GOGC"
    30→)
    31→
    32→var (
    33→	date    string
    34→	commit  string
    35→	version string
    36→)
    37→
    38→var (
    39→	flagGops, flagVersion, flagDev, flagLogDateTime bool
    40→	flagConfigFile, flagLogLevel                    string
    41→)
    42→
    43→func printVersion() {
    44→	fmt.Printf("Version:\t%s\n", version)
    45→	fmt.Printf("Git hash:\t%s\n", commit)
    46→	fmt.Printf("Build time:\t%s\n", date)
    47→}
    48→
    49→func runServer(ctx context.Context) error {
    50→	var wg sync.WaitGroup
    51→
    52→	mscfg := ccconf.GetPackageConfig("metrics")
    53→	if mscfg == nil {
    54→		return fmt.Errorf("missing metrics configuration")
    55→	}
    56→	config.InitMetrics(mscfg)
    57→
    58→	mscfg = ccconf.GetPackageConfig("metric-store")
    59→	if mscfg == nil {
    60→		return fmt.Errorf("missing metricstore configuration")
    61→	}
    62→
    63→	metricstore.Init(mscfg, config.GetMetrics(), &wg)
    64→
    65→	// Set GC percent if not configured
    66→	if os.Getenv(envGOGC) == "" {
    67→		debug.SetGCPercent(15)
    68→	}
    69→
    70→	if config.Keys.BackendURL != "" {
    71→		ms := metricstore.GetMemoryStore()
    72→		ms.SetNodeProvider(api.NewBackendNodeProvider(config.Keys.BackendURL))
    73→		cclog.Infof("Node provider configured with backend URL: %s", config.Keys.BackendURL)
    74→	}
    75→
    76→	// Initialize HTTP server
    77→	srv, err := NewServer(version, commit, date)
    78→	if err != nil {
    79→		return fmt.Errorf("creating server: %w", err)
    80→	}
    81→
    82→	// Channel to collect errors from server
    83→	errChan := make(chan error, 1)
    84→
    85→	// Start HTTP server
    86→	wg.Go(func() {
    87→		if err := srv.Start(ctx); err != nil {
    88→			errChan <- err
    89→		}
    90→	})
    91→
    92→	// Handle shutdown signals
    93→	wg.Add(1)
    94→	sigs := make(chan os.Signal, 1)
    95→	signal.Notify(sigs, syscall.SIGINT, syscall.SIGTERM)
    96→	go func() {
    97→		defer wg.Done()
    98→		select {
    99→		case <-sigs:
   100→			cclog.Info("Shutdown signal received")
   101→		case <-ctx.Done():
   102→		}
   103→
   104→		runtime.SystemdNotify(false, "Shutting down ...")
   105→		srv.Shutdown(ctx)
   106→	}()
   107→
   108→	runtime.SystemdNotify(true, "running")
   109→
   110→	// Wait for completion or errors
   111→	go func() {
   112→		wg.Wait()
   113→		close(errChan)
   114→	}()
   115→
   116→	// Wait for either server startup error or shutdown completion
   117→	if err := <-errChan; err != nil {
   118→		return err
   119→	}
   120→
   121→	cclog.Print("Graceful shutdown completed!")
   122→	return nil
   123→}
   124→
   125→func run() error {
   126→	flag.BoolVar(&flagGops, "gops", false, "Listen via github.com/google/gops/agent (for debugging)")
   127→	flag.BoolVar(&flagDev, "dev", false, "Enable development component: Swagger UI")
   128→	flag.BoolVar(&flagVersion, "version", false, "Show version information and exit")
   129→	flag.BoolVar(&flagLogDateTime, "logdate", false, "Set this flag to add date and time to log messages")
   130→	flag.StringVar(&flagConfigFile, "config", "./config.json", "Specify alternative path to `config.json`")
   131→	flag.StringVar(&flagLogLevel, "loglevel", "warn", "Sets the logging level: `[debug, info, warn (default), err, crit]`")
   132→	flag.Parse()
   133→
   134→	if flagVersion {
   135→		printVersion()
   136→		return nil
   137→	}
   138→
   139→	cclog.Init(flagLogLevel, flagLogDateTime)
   140→
   141→	if flagGops || config.Keys.Debug.EnableGops {
   142→		if err := agent.Listen(agent.Options{}); err != nil {
   143→			return fmt.Errorf("starting gops agent: %w", err)
   144→		}
   145→	}
   146→
   147→	ccconf.Init(flagConfigFile)
   148→
   149→	cfg := ccconf.GetPackageConfig("main")
   150→	if cfg == nil {
   151→		return fmt.Errorf("main configuration must be present")
   152→	}
   153→
   154→	config.Init(cfg)
   155→
   156→	natsConfig := ccconf.GetPackageConfig("nats")
   157→	if err := nats.Init(natsConfig); err != nil {
   158→		cclog.Warnf("initializing (optional) nats client: %s", err.Error())
   159→	}
   160→	nats.Connect()
   161→
   162→	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())
   163→	defer cancel()
   164→
   165→	return runServer(ctx)
   166→}
   167→
   168→func main() {
   169→	if err := run(); err != nil {
   170→		cclog.Error(err.Error())
   171→		os.Exit(1)
   172→	}
   173→}
   174→

# `cc-metric-store` version 0.1.1

This is a bugfix release of `cc-metric-store`, the metric timeseries cache
implementation of ClusterCockpit.
For release specific notes visit the [ClusterCockpit Documentation](https://clusterockpit.org/docs/release/).

Notable changes in version 0.1.0:

- Cleanup of code and restructuring
- Document REST API with Swagger
- Introduce REST API versioning
- Provide Swagger UI test web-frontend
- Introduce re-sampling of metric data
- Support also ms, ns in line protocol
- Support NATS credentials

## Breaking changes

None

     6→package config
     7→
     8→import (
     9→	"bytes"
    10→	"encoding/json"
    11→	"fmt"
    12→
    13→	"github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-backend/pkg/metricstore"
    14→	cclog "github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-lib/v2/ccLogger"
    15→)
    16→
    17→var metrics map[string]metricstore.MetricConfig
    18→
    19→type Config struct {
    20→	Address    string `json:"addr"`
    21→	CertFile   string `json:"https-cert-file"`
    22→	KeyFile    string `json:"https-key-file"`
    23→	User       string `json:"user"`
    24→	Group      string `json:"group"`
    25→	BackendURL string `json:"backend-url"`
    26→	Debug      struct {
    27→		DumpToFile string `json:"dump-to-file"`
    28→		EnableGops bool   `json:"gops"`
    29→	} `json:"debug"`
    30→	JwtPublicKey string `json:"jwt-public-key"`
    31→}
    32→
    33→var Keys Config
    34→
    35→type metricConfigJSON struct {
    36→	Frequency   int64  `json:"frequency"`
    37→	Aggregation string `json:"aggregation"`
    38→}
    39→
    40→func InitMetrics(metricConfig json.RawMessage) {
    41→	Validate(metricConfigSchema, metricConfig)
    42→
    43→	var tempMetrics map[string]metricConfigJSON
    44→	dec := json.NewDecoder(bytes.NewReader(metricConfig))
    45→	dec.DisallowUnknownFields()
    46→	if err := dec.Decode(&tempMetrics); err != nil {
    47→		cclog.Abortf("Config Init: Could not decode config file '%s'.\nError: %s\n", metricConfig, err.Error())
    48→	}
    49→
    50→	metrics = make(map[string]metricstore.MetricConfig)
    51→	for name, cfg := range tempMetrics {
    52→		agg, err := metricstore.AssignAggregationStrategy(cfg.Aggregation)
    53→		if err != nil {
    54→			cclog.Warnf("Could not parse aggregation strategy for metric '%s': %s", name, err.Error())
    55→		}
    56→		metrics[name] = metricstore.MetricConfig{
    57→			Frequency:   cfg.Frequency,
    58→			Aggregation: agg,
    59→		}
    60→	}
    61→}
    62→
    63→func Init(mainConfig json.RawMessage) {
    64→	Validate(configSchema, mainConfig)
    65→	dec := json.NewDecoder(bytes.NewReader(mainConfig))
    66→	dec.DisallowUnknownFields()
    67→	if err := dec.Decode(&Keys); err != nil {
    68→		cclog.Abortf("Config Init: Could not decode config file '%s'.\nError: %s\n", mainConfig, err.Error())
    69→	}
    70→}
    71→
    72→func GetMetricFrequency(metricName string) (int64, error) {
    73→	if metric, ok := metrics[metricName]; ok {
    74→		return metric.Frequency, nil
    75→	}
    76→	return 0, fmt.Errorf("metric %s not found", metricName)
    77→}
    78→
    79→func GetMetrics() map[string]metricstore.MetricConfig {
    80→	return metrics
    81→}

content.thinking:
Now I have a good understanding of the current codebase. Let me review the key differences from the current README:

1. **Architecture has changed**: The storage engine is now in `cc-backend/pkg/metricstore` (external package), not internal. The `internal/api/` package is the REST API wrapper.

2. **Default port**: The config shows `0.0.0.0:8082`, not `8080` as mentioned in the curl examples in the README.

3. **CLI flags**: `-dev` flag now enables Swagger UI (was mentioned before), `-gops` flag for debugging, `-logdate` for timestamps, `-version` for version info.


4. **Config and API updates**: The config now includes a `backend-url` field for the node provider, endpoints support trailing slashes for compatibility, and the healthcheck endpoint accepts JSON payloads with cluster/nodes/metrics parameters. JWT authentication has switched to Ed25519 signing, and the project depends on `cc-lib/v2`, `cc-line-protocol/v2`, and the external metricstore package. Swagger documentation is accessible at `/swagger/` when running with the `-dev` flag.

5. **Server defaults and debug features**: The default server address is `:8082` rather than `:8080`, and the debug endpoint at `GET /api/debug` accepts an optional `selector` query parameter using colon-separated values. NATS configuration now supports credentials in the config file and handles multiple cluster subscriptions.

input.content:
# ClusterCockpit Metric Store

[![Build & Test](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-store/actions/workflows/test.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-store/actions/workflows/test.yml)

The cc-metric-store provides a simple in-memory time series database for storing
metrics of cluster nodes at preconfigured intervals. It is meant to be used as
part of the [ClusterCockpit suite](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit). As all
data is kept in-memory (but written to disk as compressed JSON for long term
storage), accessing it is very fast. It also provides topology aware
aggregations over time _and_ nodes/sockets/cpus.

There are major limitations: Data only gets written to disk at periodic
checkpoints, not as soon as it is received. Also only the fixed configured
duration is stored and available.

The storage engine is provided by the
[cc-backend](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-backend) package
(`cc-backend/pkg/metricstore`). This repository provides the HTTP API wrapper.

The [NATS.io](https://nats.io/) based writing endpoint and the HTTP write
endpoint both consume messages in [this format of the InfluxDB line
protocol](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-specifications/blob/master/metrics/lineprotocol_alternative.md).

## Building

`cc-metric-store` can be built using the provided `Makefile`.
It supports the following targets:

- `make`: Build the application, copy an example configuration file and generate
  checkpoint folders if required.
- `make clean`: Clean the golang build cache and application binary
- `make distclean`: In addition to the clean target also remove the `./var`
  folder and `config.json`
- `make swagger`: Regenerate the Swagger files from the source comments.
- `make test`: Run tests and basic checks (`go build`, `go vet`, `go test`).

## Running

```sh
./cc-metric-store                              # Uses ./config.json
./cc-metric-store -config /path/to/config.json
./cc-metric-store -dev                         # Enable Swagger UI at /swagger/
./cc-metric-store -loglevel debug              # debug|info|warn (default)|err|crit
./cc-metric-store -logdate                     # Add date and time to log messages
./cc-metric-store -version                     # Show version information and exit
./cc-metric-store -gops                        # Enable gops agent for debugging
```

## REST API Endpoints

The REST API is documented in [swagger.json](./api/swagger.json). You can
explore and try the REST API using the integrated [SwaggerUI web
interface](http://localhost:8082/swagger/) (requires the `-dev` flag).

For more information on the `cc-metric-store` REST API have a look at the
ClusterCockpit documentation [website](https://clustercockpit.org/docs/reference/cc-metric-store/ccms-rest-api/).

All endpoints support both trailing-slash and non-trailing-slash variants:

| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `GET` | `/api/query/` | Query metrics with selectors |
| `POST` | `/api/write/` | Write metrics (InfluxDB line protocol) |
| `POST` | `/api/free/` | Free buffers up to a timestamp |
| `GET` | `/api/debug/` | Dump internal state |
| `GET` | `/api/healthcheck/` | Check node health status |

If `jwt-public-key` is set in `config.json`, all endpoints require JWT
authentication using an Ed25519 key (`Authorization: Bearer <token>`).

## Run tests

Some benchmarks concurrently access the `MemoryStore`, so enabling the
[Race Detector](https://golang.org/doc/articles/race_detector) might be useful.
The benchmarks also work as tests as they do check if the returned values are as
expected.

```sh
# Tests only
go test -v ./...

# Benchmarks as well
go test -bench=. -race -v ./...
```

## What are these selectors mentioned in the code?

The cc-metric-store works as a time-series database and uses the InfluxDB line
protocol as input format. Unlike InfluxDB, the data is indexed by one single
strictly hierarchical tree structure. A selector is built out of the tags in the
InfluxDB line protocol, and can be used to select a node (not in the sense of a
compute node, can also be a socket, cpu, ...) in that tree. The implementation
calls those nodes `level` to avoid confusion. It is impossible to access data
only by knowing the _socket_ or _cpu_ tag — all higher up levels have to be
specified as well.

This is what the hierarchy currently looks like:

- cluster1
  - host1
    - socket0
    - socket1
    - ...
    - cpu1
    - cpu2
    - cpu3
    - cpu4
    - ...
    - gpu1
    - gpu2
  - host2
  - ...
- cluster2
- ...

Example selectors:

1. `["cluster1", "host1", "cpu0"]`: Select only the cpu0 of host1 in cluster1
2. `["cluster1", "host1", ["cpu4", "cpu5", "cpu6", "cpu7"]]`: Select only CPUs 4-7 of host1 in cluster1
3. `["cluster1", "host1"]`: Select the complete node. If querying for a CPU-specific metric such as flops, all CPUs are implied

## Config file

The config file is a JSON document with four top-level sections.

### `main`

```json
"main": {
  "addr": "0.0.0.0:8082",
  "https-cert-file": "",
  "https-key-file": "",
  "jwt-public-key": "<base64-encoded Ed25519 public key>",
  "user": "",
  "group": "",
  "backend-url": ""
}
```

- `addr`: Address and port to listen on (default: `0.0.0.0:8082`)
- `https-cert-file` / `https-key-file`: Paths to TLS certificate/key for HTTPS
- `jwt-public-key`: Base64-encoded Ed25519 public key for JWT authentication. If empty, no auth is required.
- `user` / `group`: Drop privileges to this user/group after startup
- `backend-url`: Optional URL of a cc-backend instance used as node provider

### `metrics`

Per-metric configuration. Each key is the metric name:

```json
"metrics": {
  "cpu_load": { "frequency": 60, "aggregation": null },
  "flops_any": { "frequency": 60, "aggregation": "sum" },
  "cpu_user":  { "frequency": 60, "aggregation": "avg" }
}
```

- `frequency`: Sampling interval in seconds
- `aggregation`: How to aggregate sub-level data: `"sum"`, `"avg"`, or `null` (no aggregation)

### `metric-store`

```json
"metric-store": {
  "checkpoints": {
    "interval": "12h",
    "directory": "./var/checkpoints"
  },
  "memory-cap": 100,
  "retention-in-memory": "48h",
  "cleanup": {
    "mode": "archive",
    "interval": "48h",
    "directory": "./var/archive"
  },
  "nats-subscriptions": [
    { "subscribe-to": "hpc-nats", "cluster-tag": "fritz" }
  ]
}
```

- `checkpoints.interval`: How often to write checkpoints to disk
- `checkpoints.directory`: Where to store checkpoint files
- `memory-cap`: Memory cap in MB
- `retention-in-memory`: How long to keep data in memory (e.g. `"48h"`)
- `cleanup.mode`: What to do with old data: `"archive"` or `"delete"`
- `cleanup.interval`: How often to run the cleanup
- `cleanup.directory`: Where to archive old data
- `nats-subscriptions`: List of NATS subjects to subscribe to, with associated cluster tag

### `nats`

```json
"nats": {
  "address": "nats://0.0.0.0:4222",
  "username": "root",
  "password": "root"
}
```

NATS connection is optional. If not configured, only the HTTP write endpoint is available.

For more information see the ClusterCockpit documentation [website](https://clustercockpit.org/docs/reference/cc-metric-store/ccms-configuration/).

## Test the complete setup (excluding cc-backend itself)

There are two ways for sending data to the cc-metric-store, both of which are
supported by the
[cc-metric-collector](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-collector).
This example uses NATS; the alternative is to use HTTP.

```sh
# Only needed once, downloads the docker image
docker pull nats:latest

# Start the NATS server
docker run -p 4222:4222 -ti nats:latest
```

Second, build and start the
[cc-metric-collector](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-collector)
using the following as Sink-Config:

```json
{
  "type": "nats",
  "host": "localhost",
  "port": "4222",
  "database": "updates"
}
```

Third, build and start the metric store. For this example here, the
`config.json` file already in the repository should work just fine.

```sh
# Assuming you have a clone of this repo in ./cc-metric-store:
cd cc-metric-store
make
./cc-metric-store
```

And finally, use the API to fetch some data. The API is protected by JWT based
authentication if `jwt-public-key` is set in `config.json`. You can use this JWT
for testing:
`REDACTED`

```sh
JWT="REDACTED"

# If the collector and store and nats-server have been running for at least 60 seconds on the same host:
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JWT" \
     "http://localhost:8082/api/query/" \
     -d '{
       "cluster": "testcluster",
       "from": '"$(expr $(date +%s) - 60)"',
       "to": '"$(date +%s)"',
       "queries": [{ "metric": "cpu_load", "host": "'"$(hostname)"'" }]
     }'
```

For debugging, the debug endpoint dumps the current content to stdout:

```sh
JWT="REDACTED"

# Dump everything
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JWT" "http://localhost:8082/api/debug/"

# Dump a specific selector (colon-separated path)
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JWT" "http://localhost:8082/api/debug/?selector=testcluster:host1"
```

The cc-metric-store works as a time-series database and uses the InfluxDB line
protocol as input format. Unlike InfluxDB, the data is indexed by one single
strictly hierarchical tree structure. A selector is build out of the tags in the
InfluxDB line protocol, and can be used to select a node (not in the sense of a
compute node, can also be a socket, cpu, ...) in that tree. The implementation
calls those nodes `level` to avoid confusion. It is impossible to access data
only by knowing the _socket_ or _cpu_ tag, all higher up levels have to be
specified as well.

This is what the hierarchy currently looks like:

- cluster1
  - host1
    - socket0
    - socket1
    - ...
    - cpu1
    - cpu2
    - cpu3
    - cpu4
    - ...
    - gpu1
    - gpu2
  - host2
  - ...
- cluster2
- ...

Example selectors:

1. `["cluster1", "host1", "cpu0"]`: Select only the cpu0 of host1 in cluster1
2. `["cluster1", "host1", ["cpu4", "cpu5", "cpu6", "cpu7"]]`: Select only CPUs 4-7 of host1 in cluster1
3. `["cluster1", "host1"]`: Select the complete node. If querying for a CPU-specific metric such as floats, all CPUs are implied

## Config file

You find the configuration options on the ClusterCockpit [website](https://clustercockpit.org/docs/reference/cc-metric-store/ccms-configuration/).

## Test the complete setup (excluding cc-backend itself)

There are two ways for sending data to the cc-metric-store, both of which are
supported by the
[cc-metric-collector](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-collector).
This example uses NATS, the alternative is to use HTTP.

```sh
# Only needed once, downloads the docker image
docker pull nats:latest

# Start the NATS server
docker run -p 4222:4222 -ti nats:latest
```

Second, build and start the
[cc-metric-collector](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-collector)
using the following as Sink-Config:

```json
{
  "type": "nats",
  "host": "localhost",
  "port": "4222",
  "database": "updates"
}
```

Third, build and start the metric store. For this example here, the
`config.json` file already in the repository should work just fine.

```sh
# Assuming you have a clone of this repo in ./cc-metric-store:
cd cc-metric-store
make
./cc-metric-store
```

And finally, use the API to fetch some data. The API is protected by JWT based
authentication if `jwt-public-key` is set in `config.json`. You can use this JWT
for testing:
`REDACTED`

```sh
JWT="REDACTED"

# If the collector and store and nats-server have been running for at least 60 seconds on the same host, you may run:
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JWT" -D - "http://localhost:8080/api/query" -d "{ \"cluster\": \"testcluster\", \"from\": $(expr $(date +%s) - 60), \"to\": $(date +%s), \"queries\": [{
  \"metric\": \"load_one\",
  \"host\": \"$(hostname)\"
}] }"

# ...
```

For debugging there is a debug endpoint to dump the current content to stdout:

```sh
JWT="REDACTED"

# If the collector and store and nats-server have been running for at least 60 seconds on the same host, you may run:
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JWT" -D - "http://localhost:8080/api/debug"

# ...
```

     6→// Package metricstore provides walCheckpoint.go: WAL-based checkpoint implementation.
     7→//
     8→// This replaces the Avro shadow tree with an append-only Write-Ahead Log (WAL)
     9→// per host, eliminating the extra memory overhead of the AvroStore and providing
    10→// truly continuous (per-write) crash safety.
    11→//
    12→// # Architecture
    13→//
    14→//	Metric write (DecodeLine)
    15→//	      │
    16→//	      ├─► WriteToLevel()    → main MemoryStore (unchanged)
    17→//	      │
    18→//	      └─► WALMessages channel
    19→//	               │
    20→//	               ▼
    21→//	        WALStaging goroutine
    22→//	               │
    23→//	               ▼
    24→//	        checkpoints/cluster/host/current.wal  (append-only, binary)
    25→//
    26→//	Periodic checkpoint (Checkpointing goroutine):
    27→//	  1. Write <timestamp>.bin snapshot (column-oriented, from main tree)
    28→//	  2. Signal WALStaging to truncate current.wal per host
    29→//
    30→//	On restart (FromCheckpoint):
    31→//	  1. Load most recent <timestamp>.bin snapshot
    32→//	  2. Replay current.wal (overwrite-safe: buffer.write handles duplicate timestamps)
    33→//
    34→// # WAL Record Format
    35→//
    36→//	[4B magic 0xCC1DA7A1][4B payload_len][payload][4B CRC32]
    37→//
    38→//	payload:
    39→//	  [8B timestamp int64]
    40→//	  [2B metric_name_len uint16][N metric name bytes]
    41→//	  [1B selector_count uint8]
    42→//	  per selector: [1B selector_len uint8][M selector bytes]
    43→//	  [4B value float32 bits]
    44→//
    45→// # Binary Snapshot Format
    46→//
    47→//	[4B magic 0xCC5B0001][8B from int64][8B to int64]
    48→//	Level tree (recursive):
    49→//	  [4B num_metrics uint32]
    50→//	  per metric:
    51→//	    [2B name_len uint16][N name bytes]
    52→//	    [8B frequency int64][8B start int64]
    53→//	    [4B num_values uint32][num_values × 4B float32]
    54→//	  [4B num_children uint32]
    55→//	  per child: [2B name_len uint16][N name bytes] + Level (recursive)
    56→package metricstore
    57→
    58→import (
    59→	"bufio"
    60→	"context"
    61→	"encoding/binary"
    62→	"fmt"
    63→	"hash/crc32"
    64→	"io"
    65→	"math"
    66→	"os"
    67→	"path"
    68→	"strings"
    69→	"sync"
    70→	"sync/atomic"
    71→
    72→	cclog "github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-lib/v2/ccLogger"
    73→	"github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-lib/v2/schema"
    74→)
    75→
    76→// Magic numbers for binary formats.
    77→const (
    78→	walFileMagic   = uint32(0xCC1DA701) // WAL file header magic
    79→	walRecordMagic = uint32(0xCC1DA7A1) // WAL record magic
    80→	snapFileMagic  = uint32(0xCC5B0001) // Binary snapshot magic
    81→)
    82→
    83→// WALMessages is the channel for sending metric writes to the WAL staging goroutine.
    84→// Buffered to allow burst writes without blocking the metric ingestion path.
    85→var WALMessages = make(chan *WALMessage, 4096)
    86→
    87→// walRotateCh is used by the checkpoint goroutine to request WAL file rotation
    88→// (close, delete, reopen) after a binary snapshot has been written.
    89→var walRotateCh = make(chan walRotateReq, 256)
    90→
    91→// WALMessage represents a single metric write to be appended to the WAL.
    92→// Cluster and Node are NOT stored in the WAL record (inferred from file path).
    93→type WALMessage struct {
    94→	MetricName string
    95→	Cluster    string
    96→	Node       string
    97→	Selector   []string
    98→	Value      schema.Float
    99→	Timestamp  int64
   100→}
   101→
   102→// walRotateReq requests WAL file rotation for a specific host directory.
   103→// The done channel is closed by the WAL goroutine when rotation is complete.
   104→type walRotateReq struct {
   105→	hostDir string
   106→	done    chan struct{}
   107→}
   108→
   109→// walFileState holds an open WAL file handle for one host directory.
   110→type walFileState struct {
   111→	f *os.File
   112→}
   113→
   114→// WALStaging starts a background goroutine that receives WALMessage items
   115→// and appends binary WAL records to per-host current.wal files.
   116→// Also handles WAL rotation requests from the checkpoint goroutine.
   117→func WALStaging(wg *sync.WaitGroup, ctx context.Context) {
   118→	wg.Go(func() {
   119→		if Keys.Checkpoints.FileFormat == "json" {
   120→			return
   121→		}
   122→
   123→		hostFiles := make(map[string]*walFileState)
   124→
   125→		defer func() {
   126→			for _, ws := range hostFiles {
   127→				if ws.f != nil {
   128→					ws.f.Close()
   129→				}
   130→			}
   131→		}()
   132→
   133→		getOrOpenWAL := func(hostDir string) *os.File {
   134→			ws, ok := hostFiles[hostDir]
   135→			if ok {
   136→				return ws.f
   137→			}
   138→
   139→			if err := os.MkdirAll(hostDir, CheckpointDirPerms); err != nil {
   140→				cclog.Errorf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL: mkdir %s: %v", hostDir, err)
   141→				return nil
   142→			}
   143→
   144→			walPath := path.Join(hostDir, "current.wal")
   145→			f, err := os.OpenFile(walPath, os.O_CREATE|os.O_APPEND|os.O_WRONLY, CheckpointFilePerms)
   146→			if err != nil {
   147→				cclog.Errorf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL: open %s: %v", walPath, err)
   148→				return nil
   149→			}
   150→
   151→			// Write file header magic if file is new (empty).
   152→			info, err := f.Stat()
   153→			if err == nil && info.Size() == 0 {
   154→				var hdr [4]byte
   155→				binary.LittleEndian.PutUint32(hdr[:], walFileMagic)
   156→				if _, err := f.Write(hdr[:]); err != nil {
   157→					cclog.Errorf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL: write header %s: %v", walPath, err)
   158→					f.Close()
   159→					return nil
   160→				}
   161→			}
   162→
   163→			hostFiles[hostDir] = &walFileState{f: f}
   164→			return f
   165→		}
   166→
   167→		processMsg := func(msg *WALMessage) {
   168→			hostDir := path.Join(Keys.Checkpoints.RootDir, msg.Cluster, msg.Node)
   169→			f := getOrOpenWAL(hostDir)
   170→			if f == nil {
   171→				return
   172→			}
   173→			if err := writeWALRecord(f, msg); err != nil {
   174→				cclog.Errorf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL: write record: %v", err)
   175→			}
   176→		}
   177→
   178→		processRotate := func(req walRotateReq) {
   179→			ws, ok := hostFiles[req.hostDir]
   180→			if ok && ws.f != nil {
   181→				ws.f.Close()
   182→				walPath := path.Join(req.hostDir, "current.wal")
   183→				if err := os.Remove(walPath); err != nil && !os.IsNotExist(err) {
   184→					cclog.Errorf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL: remove %s: %v", walPath, err)
   185→				}
   186→				delete(hostFiles, req.hostDir)
   187→			}
   188→			close(req.done)
   189→		}
   190→
   191→		drain := func() {
   192→			for {
   193→				select {
   194→				case msg, ok := <-WALMessages:
   195→					if !ok {
   196→						return
   197→					}
   198→					processMsg(msg)
   199→				case req := <-walRotateCh:
   200→					processRotate(req)
   201→				default:
   202→					return
   203→				}
   204→			}
   205→		}
   206→
   207→		for {
   208→			select {
   209→			case <-ctx.Done():
   210→				drain()
   211→				return
   212→			case msg, ok := <-WALMessages:
   213→				if !ok {
   214→					return
   215→				}
   216→				processMsg(msg)
   217→			case req := <-walRotateCh:
   218→				processRotate(req)
   219→			}
   220→		}
   221→	})
   222→}
   223→
   224→// RotateWALFiles sends rotation requests for the given host directories
   225→// and blocks until all rotations complete.
   226→func RotateWALFiles(hostDirs []string) {
   227→	dones := make([]chan struct{}, len(hostDirs))
   228→	for i, dir := range hostDirs {
   229→		dones[i] = make(chan struct{})
   230→		walRotateCh <- walRotateReq{hostDir: dir, done: dones[i]}
   231→	}
   232→	for _, done := range dones {
   233→		<-done
   234→	}
   235→}
   236→
   237→// RotateWALFiles sends rotation requests for the given host directories
   238→// and blocks until all rotations complete.
   239→func RotateWALFilesAfterShutdown(hostDirs []string) {
   240→	for _, dir := range hostDirs {
   241→		walPath := path.Join(dir, "current.wal")
   242→		if err := os.Remove(walPath); err != nil && !os.IsNotExist(err) {
   243→			cclog.Errorf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL: remove %s: %v", walPath, err)
   244→		}
   245→	}
   246→}
   247→
   248→// buildWALPayload encodes a WALMessage into a binary payload (without magic/length/CRC).
   249→func buildWALPayload(msg *WALMessage) []byte {
   250→	size := 8 + 2 + len(msg.MetricName) + 1 + 4
   251→	for _, s := range msg.Selector {
   252→		size += 1 + len(s)
   253→	}
   254→
   255→	buf := make([]byte, 0, size)
   256→
   257→	// Timestamp (8 bytes, little-endian int64)
   258→	var ts [8]byte
   259→	binary.LittleEndian.PutUint64(ts[:], uint64(msg.Timestamp))
   260→	buf = append(buf, ts[:]...)
   261→
   262→	// Metric name (2-byte length prefix + bytes)
   263→	var mLen [2]byte
   264→	binary.LittleEndian.PutUint16(mLen[:], uint16(len(msg.MetricName)))
   265→	buf = append(buf, mLen[:]...)
   266→	buf = append(buf, msg.MetricName...)
   267→
   268→	// Selector count (1 byte)
   269→	buf = append(buf, byte(len(msg.Selector)))
   270→
   271→	// Selectors (1-byte length prefix + bytes each)
   272→	for _, sel := range msg.Selector {
   273→		buf = append(buf, byte(len(sel)))
   274→		buf = append(buf, sel...)
   275→	}
   276→
   277→	// Value (4 bytes, float32 bit representation)
   278→	var val [4]byte
   279→	binary.LittleEndian.PutUint32(val[:], math.Float32bits(float32(msg.Value)))
   280→	buf = append(buf, val[:]...)
   281→
   282→	return buf
   283→}
   284→
   285→// writeWALRecord appends a binary WAL record to the file.
   286→// Format: [4B magic][4B payload_len][payload][4B CRC32]
   287→func writeWALRecord(f *os.File, msg *WALMessage) error {
   288→	payload := buildWALPayload(msg)
   289→	crc := crc32.ChecksumIEEE(payload)
   290→
   291→	record := make([]byte, 0, 4+4+len(payload)+4)
   292→
   293→	var magic [4]byte
   294→	binary.LittleEndian.PutUint32(magic[:], walRecordMagic)
   295→	record = append(record, magic[:]...)
   296→
   297→	var pLen [4]byte
   298→	binary.LittleEndian.PutUint32(pLen[:], uint32(len(payload)))
   299→	record = append(record, pLen[:]...)
   300→
   301→	record = append(record, payload...)
   302→
   303→	var crcBytes [4]byte
   304→	binary.LittleEndian.PutUint32(crcBytes[:], crc)
   305→	record = append(record, crcBytes[:]...)
   306→
   307→	_, err := f.Write(record)
   308→	return err
   309→}
   310→
   311→// readWALRecord reads one WAL record from the reader.
   312→// Returns (nil, nil) on clean EOF. Returns error on data corruption.
   313→// A CRC mismatch indicates a truncated trailing record (expected on crash).
   314→func readWALRecord(r io.Reader) (*WALMessage, error) {
   315→	var magic uint32
   316→	if err := binary.Read(r, binary.LittleEndian, &magic); err != nil {
   317→		if err == io.EOF {
   318→			return nil, nil // Clean EOF
   319→		}
   320→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("read record magic: %w", err)
   321→	}
   322→
   323→	if magic != walRecordMagic {
   324→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("invalid record magic 0x%08X (expected 0x%08X)", magic, walRecordMagic)
   325→	}
   326→
   327→	var payloadLen uint32
   328→	if err := binary.Read(r, binary.LittleEndian, &payloadLen); err != nil {
   329→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("read payload length: %w", err)
   330→	}
   331→
   332→	if payloadLen > 1<<20 { // 1 MB sanity limit
   333→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("record payload too large: %d bytes", payloadLen)
   334→	}
   335→
   336→	payload := make([]byte, payloadLen)
   337→	if _, err := io.ReadFull(r, payload); err != nil {
   338→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("read payload: %w", err)
   339→	}
   340→
   341→	var storedCRC uint32
   342→	if err := binary.Read(r, binary.LittleEndian, &storedCRC); err != nil {
   343→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("read CRC: %w", err)
   344→	}
   345→
   346→	if crc32.ChecksumIEEE(payload) != storedCRC {
   347→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("CRC mismatch (truncated write or corruption)")
   348→	}
   349→
   350→	return parseWALPayload(payload)
   351→}
   352→
   353→// parseWALPayload decodes a binary payload into a WALMessage.
   354→func parseWALPayload(payload []byte) (*WALMessage, error) {
   355→	if len(payload) < 8+2+1+4 {
   356→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("payload too short: %d bytes", len(payload))
   357→	}
   358→
   359→	offset := 0
   360→
   361→	// Timestamp (8 bytes)
   362→	ts := int64(binary.LittleEndian.Uint64(payload[offset : offset+8]))
   363→	offset += 8
   364→
   365→	// Metric name (2-byte length + bytes)
   366→	if offset+2 > len(payload) {
   367→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("metric name length overflows payload")
   368→	}
   369→	mLen := int(binary.LittleEndian.Uint16(payload[offset : offset+2]))
   370→	offset += 2
   371→
   372→	if offset+mLen > len(payload) {
   373→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("metric name overflows payload")
   374→	}
   375→	metricName := string(payload[offset : offset+mLen])
   376→	offset += mLen
   377→
   378→	// Selector count (1 byte)
   379→	if offset >= len(payload) {
   380→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("selector count overflows payload")
   381→	}
   382→	selCount := int(payload[offset])
   383→	offset++
   384→
   385→	selectors := make([]string, selCount)
   386→	for i := range selCount {
   387→		if offset >= len(payload) {
   388→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("selector[%d] length overflows payload", i)
   389→		}
   390→		sLen := int(payload[offset])
   391→		offset++
   392→
   393→		if offset+sLen > len(payload) {
   394→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("selector[%d] data overflows payload", i)
   395→		}
   396→		selectors[i] = string(payload[offset : offset+sLen])
   397→		offset += sLen
   398→	}
   399→
   400→	// Value (4 bytes, float32 bits)
   401→	if offset+4 > len(payload) {
   402→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("value overflows payload")
   403→	}
   404→	bits := binary.LittleEndian.Uint32(payload[offset : offset+4])
   405→	value := schema.Float(math.Float32frombits(bits))
   406→
   407→	return &WALMessage{
   408→		MetricName: metricName,
   409→		Timestamp:  ts,
   410→		Selector:   selectors,
   411→		Value:      value,
   412→	}, nil
   413→}
   414→
   415→// loadWALFile reads a WAL file and replays all valid records into the Level tree.
   416→// l is the host-level node. Corrupt or partial trailing records are silently skipped
   417→// (expected on crash). Records older than 'from' are skipped.
   418→func (l *Level) loadWALFile(m *MemoryStore, f *os.File, from int64) error {
   419→	br := bufio.NewReader(f)
   420→
   421→	// Verify file header magic.
   422→	var fileMagic uint32
   423→	if err := binary.Read(br, binary.LittleEndian, &fileMagic); err != nil {
   424→		if err == io.EOF {
   425→			return nil // Empty file, no data
   426→		}
   427→		return fmt.Errorf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL: read file header: %w", err)
   428→	}
   429→
   430→	if fileMagic != walFileMagic {
   431→		return fmt.Errorf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL: invalid file magic 0x%08X (expected 0x%08X)", fileMagic, walFileMagic)
   432→	}
   433→
   434→	// Cache level lookups to avoid repeated tree traversal.
   435→	lvlCache := make(map[string]*Level)
   436→
   437→	for {
   438→		msg, err := readWALRecord(br)
   439→		if err != nil {
   440→			// Truncated trailing record is expected after a crash; stop replaying.
   441→			cclog.Debugf("[METRICSTORE]> WAL: stopping replay at corrupted/partial record: %v", err)
   442→			break
   443→		}
   444→		if msg == nil {
   445→			break // Clean EOF
   446→		}
   447→
   448→		if msg.Timestamp < from {
   449→			continue // Older than retention window
   450→		}
   451→
   452→		minfo, ok := m.Metrics[msg.MetricName]
   453→		if !ok {
   454→			continue // Unknown metric (config may have changed)
   455→		}
   456→
   457→		// Cache key is the null-separated selector path.
   458→		cacheKey := joinSelector(msg.Selector)
   459→		lvl, ok := lvlCache[cacheKey]
   460→		if !ok {
   461→			lvl = l.findLevelOrCreate(msg.Selector, len(m.Metrics))
   462→			lvlCache[cacheKey] = lvl
   463→		}
   464→
   465→		// Write directly to the buffer, same as WriteToLevel but without the
   466→		// global level lookup (we already have the right level).
   467→		lvl.lock.Lock()
   468→		b := lvl.metrics[minfo.offset]
   469→		if b == nil {
   470→			b = newBuffer(msg.Timestamp, minfo.Frequency)
   471→			lvl.metrics[minfo.offset] = b
   472→		}
   473→		nb, writeErr := b.write(msg.Timestamp, msg.Value)
   474→		if writeErr == nil && b != nb {
   475→			lvl.metrics[minfo.offset] = nb
   476→		}
   477→		// Ignore write errors for timestamps before buffer start (can happen when
   478→		// replaying WAL entries that predate a loaded snapshot's start time).
   479→		lvl.lock.Unlock()
   480→	}
   481→
   482→	return nil
   483→}
   484→
   485→// joinSelector builds a cache key from a selector slice using null bytes as separators.
   486→func joinSelector(sel []string) string {
   487→	if len(sel) == 0 {
   488→		return ""
   489→	}
   490→	var result strings.Builder
   491→	result.WriteString(sel[0])
   492→	for i := 1; i < len(sel); i++ {
   493→		result.WriteString("\x00" + sel[i])
   494→	}
   495→	return result.String()
   496→}

   498→// ToCheckpointWAL writes binary snapshot files for all hosts in parallel.
   499→// Returns the number of files written, the list of host directories that were
   500→// successfully checkpointed (for WAL rotation), and any errors.
   501→func (m *MemoryStore) ToCheckpointWAL(dir string, from, to int64) (int, []string, error) {
   502→	// Collect all cluster/host pairs.
   503→	m.root.lock.RLock()
   504→	totalHosts := 0
   505→	for _, l1 := range m.root.children {
   506→		l1.lock.RLock()
   507→		totalHosts += len(l1.children)
   508→		l1.lock.RUnlock()
   509→	}
   510→	m.root.lock.RUnlock()
   511→
   512→	levels := make([]*Level, 0, totalHosts)
   513→	selectors := make([][]string, 0, totalHosts)
   514→
   515→	m.root.lock.RLock()
   516→	for sel1, l1 := range m.root.children {
   517→		l1.lock.RLock()
   518→		for sel2, l2 := range l1.children {
   519→			levels = append(levels, l2)
   520→			selectors = append(selectors, []string{sel1, sel2})
   521→		}
   522→		l1.lock.RUnlock()
   523→	}
   524→	m.root.lock.RUnlock()
   525→
   526→	type workItem struct {
   527→		level    *Level
   528→		hostDir  string
   529→		selector []string
   530→	}
   531→
   532→	n, errs := int32(0), int32(0)
   533→	var successDirs []string
   534→	var successMu sync.Mutex
   535→
   536→	var wg sync.WaitGroup
   537→	wg.Add(Keys.NumWorkers)
   538→	work := make(chan workItem, Keys.NumWorkers*2)
   539→
   540→	for range Keys.NumWorkers {
   541→		go func() {
   542→			defer wg.Done()
   543→			for wi := range work {
   544→				err := wi.level.toCheckpointBinary(wi.hostDir, from, to, m)
   545→				if err != nil {
   546→					if err == ErrNoNewArchiveData {
   547→						continue
   548→					}
   549→					cclog.Errorf("[METRICSTORE]> binary checkpoint error for %s: %v", wi.hostDir, err)
   550→					atomic.AddInt32(&errs, 1)
   551→				} else {
   552→					atomic.AddInt32(&n, 1)
   553→					successMu.Lock()
   554→					successDirs = append(successDirs, wi.hostDir)
   555→					successMu.Unlock()
   556→				}
   557→			}
   558→		}()
   559→	}
   560→
   561→	for i := range levels {
   562→		hostDir := path.Join(dir, path.Join(selectors[i]...))
   563→		work <- workItem{
   564→			level:    levels[i],
   565→			hostDir:  hostDir,
   566→			selector: selectors[i],
   567→		}
   568→	}
   569→	close(work)
   570→	wg.Wait()
   571→
   572→	if errs > 0 {
   573→		return int(n), successDirs, fmt.Errorf("[METRICSTORE]> %d errors during binary checkpoint (%d successes)", errs, n)
   574→	}
   575→	return int(n), successDirs, nil
   576→}
   577→
   578→// toCheckpointBinary writes a binary snapshot file for a single host-level node.
   579→// Uses atomic rename (write to .tmp then rename) to avoid partial reads on crash.
   580→func (l *Level) toCheckpointBinary(dir string, from, to int64, m *MemoryStore) error {
   581→	cf, err := l.toCheckpointFile(from, to, m)
   582→	if err != nil {
   583→		return err
   584→	}
   585→	if cf == nil {
   586→		return ErrNoNewArchiveData
   587→	}
   588→
   589→	if err := os.MkdirAll(dir, CheckpointDirPerms); err != nil {
   590→		return fmt.Errorf("mkdir %s: %w", dir, err)
   591→	}
   592→
   593→	// Write to a temp file first, then rename (atomic on POSIX).
   594→	tmpPath := path.Join(dir, fmt.Sprintf("%d.bin.tmp", from))
   595→	finalPath := path.Join(dir, fmt.Sprintf("%d.bin", from))
   596→
   597→	f, err := os.OpenFile(tmpPath, os.O_CREATE|os.O_WRONLY|os.O_TRUNC, CheckpointFilePerms)
   598→	if err != nil {
   599→		return fmt.Errorf("open binary snapshot %s: %w", tmpPath, err)
   600→	}
   601→
   602→	bw := bufio.NewWriter(f)
   603→	if err := writeBinarySnapshotFile(bw, cf); err != nil {
   604→		f.Close()
   605→		os.Remove(tmpPath)
   606→		return fmt.Errorf("write binary snapshot: %w", err)
   607→	}
   608→	if err := bw.Flush(); err != nil {
   609→		f.Close()
   610→		os.Remove(tmpPath)
   611→		return err
   612→	}
   613→	f.Close()
   614→
   615→	return os.Rename(tmpPath, finalPath)
   616→}
   617→
   618→// writeBinarySnapshotFile writes the binary snapshot file header and level tree.
   619→func writeBinarySnapshotFile(w io.Writer, cf *CheckpointFile) error {
   620→	if err := binary.Write(w, binary.LittleEndian, snapFileMagic); err != nil {
   621→		return err
   622→	}
   623→	if err := binary.Write(w, binary.LittleEndian, cf.From); err != nil {
   624→		return err
   625→	}
   626→	if err := binary.Write(w, binary.LittleEndian, cf.To); err != nil {
   627→		return err
   628→	}
   629→	return writeBinaryLevel(w, cf)
   630→}
   631→
   632→// writeBinaryLevel recursively writes a CheckpointFile level in binary format.
   633→func writeBinaryLevel(w io.Writer, cf *CheckpointFile) error {
   634→	if err := binary.Write(w, binary.LittleEndian, uint32(len(cf.Metrics))); err != nil {
   635→		return err
   636→	}
   637→
   638→	for name, metric := range cf.Metrics {
   639→		if err := writeString16(w, name); err != nil {
   640→			return err
   641→		}
   642→		if err := binary.Write(w, binary.LittleEndian, metric.Frequency); err != nil {
   643→			return err
   644→		}
   645→		if err := binary.Write(w, binary.LittleEndian, metric.Start); err != nil {
   646→			return err
   647→		}
   648→		if err := binary.Write(w, binary.LittleEndian, uint32(len(metric.Data))); err != nil {
   649→			return err
   650→		}
   651→		for _, v := range metric.Data {
   652→			if err := binary.Write(w, binary.LittleEndian, math.Float32bits(float32(v))); err != nil {
   653→				return err
   654→			}
   655→		}
   656→	}
   657→
   658→	if err := binary.Write(w, binary.LittleEndian, uint32(len(cf.Children))); err != nil {
   659→		return err
   660→	}
   661→
   662→	for name, child := range cf.Children {
   663→		if err := writeString16(w, name); err != nil {
   664→			return err
   665→		}
   666→		if err := writeBinaryLevel(w, child); err != nil {
   667→			return err
   668→		}
   669→	}
   670→
   671→	return nil
   672→}
   673→
   674→// writeString16 writes a 2-byte length-prefixed string to w.
   675→func writeString16(w io.Writer, s string) error {
   676→	if err := binary.Write(w, binary.LittleEndian, uint16(len(s))); err != nil {
   677→		return err
   678→	}
   679→	_, err := io.WriteString(w, s)
   680→	return err
   681→}
   682→
   683→// loadBinaryFile reads a binary snapshot file and loads data into the Level tree.
   684→// The retention check (from) is applied to the file's 'to' timestamp.
   685→func (l *Level) loadBinaryFile(m *MemoryStore, f *os.File, from int64) error {
   686→	br := bufio.NewReader(f)
   687→
   688→	var magic uint32
   689→	if err := binary.Read(br, binary.LittleEndian, &magic); err != nil {
   690→		return fmt.Errorf("[METRICSTORE]> binary snapshot: read magic: %w", err)
   691→	}
   692→	if magic != snapFileMagic {
   693→		return fmt.Errorf("[METRICSTORE]> binary snapshot: invalid magic 0x%08X (expected 0x%08X)", magic, snapFileMagic)
   694→	}
   695→
   696→	var fileFrom, fileTo int64
   697→	if err := binary.Read(br, binary.LittleEndian, &fileFrom); err != nil {
   698→		return fmt.Errorf("[METRICSTORE]> binary snapshot: read from: %w", err)
   699→	}
   700→	if err := binary.Read(br, binary.LittleEndian, &fileTo); err != nil {
   701→		return fmt.Errorf("[METRICSTORE]> binary snapshot: read to: %w", err)
   702→	}
   703→
   704→	if fileTo != 0 && fileTo < from {
   705→		return nil // File is older than retention window, skip it
   706→	}
   707→
   708→	cf, err := readBinaryLevel(br)
   709→	if err != nil {
   710→		return fmt.Errorf("[METRICSTORE]> binary snapshot: read level tree: %w", err)
   711→	}
   712→	cf.From = fileFrom
   713→	cf.To = fileTo
   714→
   715→	return l.loadFile(cf, m)
   716→}
   717→
   718→// readBinaryLevel recursively reads a level from the binary snapshot format.
   719→func readBinaryLevel(r io.Reader) (*CheckpointFile, error) {
   720→	cf := &CheckpointFile{
   721→		Metrics:  make(map[string]*CheckpointMetrics),
   722→		Children: make(map[string]*CheckpointFile),
   723→	}
   724→
   725→	var numMetrics uint32
   726→	if err := binary.Read(r, binary.LittleEndian, &numMetrics); err != nil {
   727→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("read num_metrics: %w", err)
   728→	}
   729→
   730→	for range numMetrics {
   731→		name, err := readString16(r)
   732→		if err != nil {
   733→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("read metric name: %w", err)
   734→		}
   735→
   736→		var freq, start int64
   737→		if err := binary.Read(r, binary.LittleEndian, &freq); err != nil {
   738→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("read frequency for %s: %w", name, err)
   739→		}
   740→		if err := binary.Read(r, binary.LittleEndian, &start); err != nil {
   741→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("read start for %s: %w", name, err)
   742→		}
   743→
   744→		var numValues uint32
   745→		if err := binary.Read(r, binary.LittleEndian, &numValues); err != nil {
   746→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("read num_values for %s: %w", name, err)
   747→		}
   748→
   749→		data := make([]schema.Float, numValues)
   750→		for i := range numValues {
   751→			var bits uint32
   752→			if err := binary.Read(r, binary.LittleEndian, &bits); err != nil {
   753→				return nil, fmt.Errorf("read value[%d] for %s: %w", i, name, err)
   754→			}
   755→			data[i] = schema.Float(math.Float32frombits(bits))
   756→		}
   757→
   758→		cf.Metrics[name] = &CheckpointMetrics{
   759→			Frequency: freq,
   760→			Start:     start,
   761→			Data:      data,
   762→		}
   763→	}
   764→
   765→	var numChildren uint32
   766→	if err := binary.Read(r, binary.LittleEndian, &numChildren); err != nil {
   767→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("read num_children: %w", err)
   768→	}
   769→
   770→	for range numChildren {
   771→		childName, err := readString16(r)
   772→		if err != nil {
   773→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("read child name: %w", err)
   774→		}
   775→
   776→		child, err := readBinaryLevel(r)
   777→		if err != nil {
   778→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("read child %s: %w", childName, err)
   779→		}
   780→		cf.Children[childName] = child
   781→	}
   782→
   783→	return cf, nil
   784→}
   785→
   786→// readString16 reads a 2-byte length-prefixed string from r.
   787→func readString16(r io.Reader) (string, error) {
   788→	var sLen uint16
   789→	if err := binary.Read(r, binary.LittleEndian, &sLen); err != nil {
   790→		return "", err
   791→	}
   792→	buf := make([]byte, sLen)
   793→	if _, err := io.ReadFull(r, buf); err != nil {
   794→		return "", err
   795→	}
   796→	return string(buf), nil
   797→}

     6→// Package metricstore provides config.go: Configuration structures and metric management.
     7→//
     8→// # Configuration Hierarchy
     9→//
    10→// The metricstore package uses nested configuration structures:
    11→//
    12→//	MetricStoreConfig (Keys)
    13→//	├─ NumWorkers: Parallel checkpoint/archive workers
    14→//	├─ RetentionInMemory: How long to keep data in RAM
    15→//	├─ MemoryCap: Memory limit in bytes (triggers forceFree)
    16→//	├─ Checkpoints: Persistence configuration
    17→//	│  ├─ FileFormat: "json" or "wal"
    18→//	│  ├─ Interval: How often to save (e.g., "1h")
    19→//	│  └─ RootDir: Checkpoint storage path
    20→//	├─ Cleanup: Long-term storage configuration
    21→//	│  ├─ Interval: How often to delete/archive
    22→//	│  ├─ RootDir: Archive storage path
    23→//	│  └─ Mode: "delete" or "archive"
    24→//	├─ Debug: Development/debugging options
    25→//	└─ Subscriptions: NATS topic subscriptions for metric ingestion
    26→//
    27→// # Metric Configuration
    28→//
    29→// Each metric (e.g., "cpu_load", "mem_used") has a MetricConfig entry in the global
    30→// Metrics map, defining:
    31→//
    32→//   - Frequency: Measurement interval in seconds
    33→//   - Aggregation: How to combine values (sum/avg/none) when transforming scopes
    34→//   - offset: Internal index into Level.metrics slice (assigned during Init)
    35→//
    36→// # AggregationStrategy
    37→//
    38→// Determines how to combine metric values when aggregating from finer to coarser scopes:
    39→//
    40→//   - NoAggregation: Do not combine (incompatible scopes)
    41→//   - SumAggregation: Add values (e.g., power consumption: core→socket)
    42→//   - AvgAggregation: Average values (e.g., temperature: core→socket)
    43→package metricstore
    44→
    45→import (
    46→	"fmt"
    47→	"time"
    48→
    49→	"github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-backend/pkg/archive"
    50→	cclog "github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-lib/v2/ccLogger"
    51→	"github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-lib/v2/schema"
    52→)
    53→
    54→const (
    55→	DefaultMaxWorkers                 = 10
    56→	DefaultBufferCapacity             = 512
    57→	DefaultGCTriggerInterval          = 100
    58→	DefaultMemoryUsageTrackerInterval = 1 * time.Hour
    59→)
    60→
    61→// Checkpoints configures periodic persistence of in-memory metric data.
    62→//
    63→// Fields:
    64→//   - FileFormat: "json" (human-readable, periodic) or "wal" (binary snapshot + WAL, crash-safe)
    65→//   - Interval:   Duration string (e.g., "1h", "30m") between checkpoint saves
    66→//   - RootDir:    Filesystem path for checkpoint files (created if missing)
    67→type Checkpoints struct {
    68→	FileFormat string `json:"file-format"`
    69→	Interval   string `json:"interval"`
    70→	RootDir    string `json:"directory"`
    71→}
    72→
    73→// Debug provides development and profiling options.
    74→//
    75→// Fields:
    76→//   - DumpToFile: Path to dump checkpoint data for inspection (empty = disabled)
    77→//   - EnableGops: Enable gops agent for live runtime debugging (https://github.com/google/gops)
    78→type Debug struct {
    79→	DumpToFile string `json:"dump-to-file"`
    80→	EnableGops bool   `json:"gops"`
    81→}
    82→
    83→// Archive configures long-term storage of old metric data.
    84→//
    85→// Data older than RetentionInMemory is archived to disk or deleted.
    86→//
    87→// Fields:
    88→//   - ArchiveInterval: Duration string (e.g., "24h") between archive operations
    89→//   - RootDir:         Filesystem path for archived data (created if missing)
    90→//   - DeleteInstead:   If true, delete old data instead of archiving (saves disk space)
    91→type Cleanup struct {
    92→	Interval string `json:"interval"`
    93→	RootDir  string `json:"directory"`
    94→	Mode     string `json:"mode"`
    95→}
    96→
    97→// Subscriptions defines NATS topics to subscribe to for metric ingestion.
    98→//
    99→// Each subscription receives metrics via NATS messaging, enabling real-time
   100→// data collection from compute nodes.
   101→//
   102→// Fields:
   103→//   - SubscribeTo: NATS subject/channel name (e.g., "metrics.compute.*")
   104→//   - ClusterTag:  Default cluster name for metrics without cluster tag (optional)
   105→type Subscriptions []struct {
   106→	// Channel name
   107→	SubscribeTo string `json:"subscribe-to"`
   108→
   109→	// Allow lines without a cluster tag, use this as default, optional
   110→	ClusterTag string `json:"cluster-tag"`
   111→}
   112→
   113→// MetricStoreConfig defines the main configuration for the metricstore.
   114→//
   115→// Loaded from cc-backend's config.json "metricstore" section. Controls memory usage,
   116→// persistence, archiving, and metric ingestion.
   117→//
   118→// Fields:
   119→//   - NumWorkers:        Parallel workers for checkpoint/archive (0 = auto: min(NumCPU/2+1, 10))
   120→//   - RetentionInMemory: Duration string (e.g., "48h") for in-memory data retention
   121→//   - MemoryCap:         Max bytes for buffer data (0 = unlimited); triggers forceFree when exceeded
   122→//   - Checkpoints:       Periodic persistence configuration
   123→//   - Debug:             Development/profiling options (nil = disabled)
   124→//   - Archive:           Long-term storage configuration (nil = disabled)
   125→//   - Subscriptions:     NATS topics for metric ingestion (nil = polling only)
   126→type MetricStoreConfig struct {
   127→	// Number of concurrent workers for checkpoint and archive operations.
   128→	// If not set or 0, defaults to min(runtime.NumCPU()/2+1, 10)
   129→	NumWorkers        int            `json:"num-workers"`
   130→	RetentionInMemory string         `json:"retention-in-memory"`
   131→	MemoryCap         int            `json:"memory-cap"`
   132→	Checkpoints       Checkpoints    `json:"checkpoints"`
   133→	Debug             *Debug         `json:"debug"`
   134→	Cleanup           *Cleanup       `json:"cleanup"`
   135→	Subscriptions     *Subscriptions `json:"nats-subscriptions"`
   136→}
   137→
   138→// Keys is the global metricstore configuration instance.

   152→// AggregationStrategy defines how to combine metric values across hierarchy levels.

   166→	AvgAggregation                            // Average values (e.g., temperature, utilization)

   175→//
   176→// Returns:

   179→func AssignAggregationStrategy(str string) (AggregationStrategy, error) {
   180→	switch str {
   181→	case "":
   182→		return NoAggregation, nil
   183→	case "sum":
   184→		return SumAggregation, nil
   185→	case "avg":
   186→		return AvgAggregation, nil

## Comprehensive Report: cc-backend Metricstore WAL & Parquet Architecture

Based on my exploration of the cc-backend metricstore package (version 1.4.5-0.20260303062012-8b0f63b1f849), here are the detailed findings:

---

### 1. WAL (Write-Ahead Log) CHECKPOINT FORMAT

#### File Structure and Magic Numbers
Located in: `/Users/jan/go/pkg/mod/github.com/!cluster!cockpit/cc-backend@v1.4.5-0.20260303062012-8b0f63b1f849/pkg/metricstore/walCheckpoint.go`

**Magic Numbers:**
```go
walFileMagic   = uint32(0xCC1DA701) // WAL file header magic
walRecordMagic = uint32(0xCC1DA7A1) // WAL record magic
snapFileMagic  = uint32(0xCC5B0001) // Binary snapshot magic
```

#### WAL Record Format (per-write entries)
```
[4B magic 0xCC1DA7A1][4B payload_len][payload][4B CRC32]

payload structure:
  [8B timestamp int64]
  [2B metric_name_len uint16][N metric name bytes]
  [1B selector_count uint8]
  per selector: [1B selector_len uint8][M selector bytes]
  [4B value float32 bits]
```

**Key Features:**
- Append-only binary format (per-write crash safety)
- CRC32 checksums for data integrity
- File-level magic header
- Truncated trailing records (from crashes) are silently skipped
- Stored at: `checkpoints/cluster/host/current.wal`

#### Binary Snapshot Format (periodic periodic snapshots)
```
[4B magic 0xCC5B0001][8B from int64][8B to int64]

Level tree (recursive):
  [4B num_metrics uint32]
  per metric:
    [2B name_len uint16][N name bytes]
    [8B frequency int64][8B start int64]
    [4B num_values uint32][num_values × 4B float32]
  [4B num_children uint32]
  per child: [2B name_len uint16][N name bytes] + Level (recursive)
```

**Key Features:**
- Column-oriented hierarchical structure
- Atomic writes (write to .tmp, then rename)
- Supports all metric frequencies and time ranges
- Stored at: `checkpoints/cluster/host/TIMESTAMP.bin`

#### Data Structures
```go
type WALMessage struct {
    MetricName string       // Metric name (no cluster/node, inferred from file path)
    Cluster    string       // Cluster name
    Node       string       // Node/host name
    Selector   []string     // Path selector (e.g., ["socket0", "core1"])
    Value      schema.Float // Metric value
    Timestamp  int64        // Unix timestamp
}

type CheckpointMetrics struct {
    Data      []schema.Float `json:"data"`
    Frequency int64          `json:"frequency"`
    Start     int64          `json:"start"`
}

type CheckpointFile struct {
    Metrics  map[string]*CheckpointMetrics `json:"metrics"`
    Children map[string]*CheckpointFile    `json:"children"`
    From     int64                         `json:"from"`
    To       int64                         `json:"to"`
}
```

#### Checkpoint Workflow
1. **Init (startup)**: `FromCheckpointFiles()` loads checkpoints within retention window
2. **Checkpointing (background)**: 
   - JSON mode: `ToCheckpoint()` periodic snapshots
   - WAL mode: `ToCheckpointWAL()` binary snapshots + WAL rotation
3. **WAL Staging**: `WALStaging()` goroutine receives `WALMessages` and appends binary records
4. **Shutdown**: Final checkpoint written, then WAL files deleted or rotated

#### Parallel Processing
- Worker pool size: configurable via `Keys.NumWorkers` (default: min(NumCPU/2+1, 10))
- Host-level locking: one host locked at a time for checkpoint writes
- Allows concurrent reads/writes to other hosts during checkpointing

---

### 2. PARQUET CLEANUP/ARCHIVE FORMAT

Located in: `/Users/jan/go/pkg/mod/github.com/!cluster!cockpit/cc-backend@v1.4.5-0.20260303062012-8b0f63b1f849/pkg/metricstore/parquetArchive.go`

#### Data Schema (Long Format)
```go
type ParquetMetricRow struct {
    Cluster   string  `parquet:"cluster"`
    Hostname  string  `parquet:"hostname"`
    Metric    string  `parquet:"metric"`
    Scope     string  `parquet:"scope"`
    ScopeID   string  `parquet:"scope_id"`
    Timestamp int64   `parquet:"timestamp"`
    Frequency int64   `parquet:"frequency"`
    Value     float32 `parquet:"value"`
}
```

**One row per data point**: (host, metric, scope, scope_id, timestamp)

#### Scope Parsing Logic
Hierarchy mapping from checkpoint tree names to scope/scope_id:
```go
prefixes := {
    "socket"        → ("socket", "ID"),
    "memoryDomain"  → ("memoryDomain", "ID"),
    "core"          → ("core", "ID"),
    "hwthread"      → ("hwthread", "ID"),
    "cpu"           → ("hwthread", "ID"),  // Legacy alias
    "accelerator"   → ("accelerator", "ID"),
    "unknown"       → ("unknown", "")
}

Examples:
  "socket0"    → ("socket", "0")
  "core12"     → ("core", "12")
  "a0"         → ("a", "")  // Unknown prefix
```

#### File Organization
```
archive/
  cluster1/
    1234567890.parquet  (all hosts in cluster1, archive timestamp)
  cluster2/
    1234567890.parquet
```

**One Parquet file per cluster**, consolidating all hosts

#### Compression & Sorting
- **Compression**: Zstandard (Zstd)
- **Write Buffer**: 1MB for performance
- **Sorting**: By (cluster, hostname, metric, timestamp) for optimal compression
- **Library**: `github.com/parquet-go/parquet-go`

#### Archive Workflow
```
1. archiveCheckpoints()
   ├─ For each cluster
   ├─ For each host (parallel, worker pool)
   │  └─ archiveCheckpointsToParquet()
   │     ├─ Read checkpoint files (JSON or binary)
   │     └─ flattenCheckpointFile() → ParquetMetricRow slice
   ├─ writeParquetArchive() → single Parquet file
   └─ Delete archived checkpoint files
```

### 4. KEY STRUCT TYPES & FILE FORMATS

#### Summary Table

|-----------|------------|--------------|-----------|--------------|

The cc-metric-store project at `/Users/jan/prg/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-store` imports and uses:

```go
    NoAggregation  = iota  // Do not aggregate
    SumAggregation         // Add values (power, energy)
    AvgAggregation         // Average values (temperature, utilization)
)
```

---

### 5. INTEGRATION WITH cc-metric-store

The cc-metric-store project at `/Users/jan/prg/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-store` imports and uses:
- Checkpoint loading at startup (JSON or WAL mode)
- Background checkpointing worker
- WAL staging for crash-safe writes
- Cleanup worker for archiving/deletion

**Entry point**: `cmd/cc-metric-store/main.go` → calls `MemoryStore.FromCheckpointFiles()` at init

---

### 6. TEST EXAMPLES

From `parquetArchive_test.go`:
- **TestParseScopeFromName**: Validates scope/scope_id extraction (socket0→socket/0, cpu0→hwthread/0)
- **TestFlattenCheckpointFile**: Converts hierarchical checkpoint to Parquet rows
- **TestParquetArchiveRoundtrip**: End-to-end checkpoint→JSON→Parquet→read verification
- **TestLoadCheckpointFileFromDisk_JSON**: Loads and validates JSON checkpoint format

---

This comprehensive architecture enables:
1. **Graceful restarts** via checkpointing (JSON for human inspection, WAL for crash safety)
2. **Long-term archival** to Parquet (compressed, queryable, one file per cluster)
3. **Memory efficiency** via retention windows and periodic cleanup
4. **Parallel processing** of hosts during checkpointing/archival operations

# ClusterCockpit Metric Store

[![Build & Test](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-store/actions/workflows/test.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-store/actions/workflows/test.yml)

The cc-metric-store provides a simple in-memory time series database for storing
metrics of cluster nodes at preconfigured intervals. It is meant to be used as
part of the [ClusterCockpit suite](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit). As all
data is kept in-memory, accessing it is very fast. It also provides topology aware
aggregations over time _and_ nodes/sockets/cpus.

There are major limitations: Data only gets written to disk at periodic
checkpoints, not as soon as it is received. Also only the fixed configured
duration is stored and available.

The storage engine is provided by the
[cc-backend](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-backend) package
(`cc-backend/pkg/metricstore`). This repository provides the HTTP API wrapper.

The [NATS.io](https://nats.io/) based writing endpoint and the HTTP write
endpoint both consume messages in [this format of the InfluxDB line
protocol](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-specifications/blob/master/metrics/lineprotocol_alternative.md).

## Building

`cc-metric-store` can be built using the provided `Makefile`.
It supports the following targets:

- `make`: Build the application, copy an example configuration file and generate
  checkpoint folders if required.
- `make clean`: Clean the golang build cache and application binary
- `make distclean`: In addition to the clean target also remove the `./var`
  folder and `config.json`
- `make swagger`: Regenerate the Swagger files from the source comments.
- `make test`: Run tests and basic checks (`go build`, `go vet`, `go test`).

## Running

```sh
./cc-metric-store                              # Uses ./config.json
./cc-metric-store -config /path/to/config.json
./cc-metric-store -dev                         # Enable Swagger UI at /swagger/
./cc-metric-store -loglevel debug              # debug|info|warn (default)|err|crit
./cc-metric-store -logdate                     # Add date and time to log messages
./cc-metric-store -version                     # Show version information and exit
./cc-metric-store -gops                        # Enable gops agent for debugging
```

## REST API Endpoints

The REST API is documented in [swagger.json](./api/swagger.json). You can
explore and try the REST API using the integrated [SwaggerUI web
interface](http://localhost:8082/swagger/) (requires the `-dev` flag).

For more information on the `cc-metric-store` REST API have a look at the
ClusterCockpit documentation [website](https://clustercockpit.org/docs/reference/cc-metric-store/ccms-rest-api/).

All endpoints support both trailing-slash and non-trailing-slash variants:

| Method | Path                | Description                            |
| ------ | ------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| `GET`  | `/api/query/`       | Query metrics with selectors           |
| `POST` | `/api/write/`       | Write metrics (InfluxDB line protocol) |
| `POST` | `/api/free/`        | Free buffers up to a timestamp         |
| `GET`  | `/api/debug/`       | Dump internal state                    |
| `GET`  | `/api/healthcheck/` | Check node health status               |

If `jwt-public-key` is set in `config.json`, all endpoints require JWT
authentication using an Ed25519 key (`Authorization: Bearer <token>`).

## Run tests

Some benchmarks concurrently access the `MemoryStore`, so enabling the
[Race Detector](https://golang.org/doc/articles/race_detector) might be useful.
The benchmarks also work as tests as they do check if the returned values are as
expected.

```sh
# Tests only
go test -v ./...

# Benchmarks as well
go test -bench=. -race -v ./...
```

## What are these selectors mentioned in the code?

The cc-metric-store works as a time-series database and uses the InfluxDB line
protocol as input format. Unlike InfluxDB, the data is indexed by one single
strictly hierarchical tree structure. A selector is built out of the tags in the
InfluxDB line protocol, and can be used to select a node (not in the sense of a
compute node, can also be a socket, cpu, ...) in that tree. The implementation
calls those nodes `level` to avoid confusion. It is impossible to access data
only by knowing the _socket_ or _cpu_ tag — all higher up levels have to be
specified as well.

This is what the hierarchy currently looks like:

- cluster1
  - host1
    - socket0
    - socket1
    - ...
    - cpu1
    - cpu2
    - cpu3
    - cpu4
    - ...
    - gpu1
    - gpu2
  - host2
  - ...
- cluster2
- ...

Example selectors:

1. `["cluster1", "host1", "cpu0"]`: Select only the cpu0 of host1 in cluster1
2. `["cluster1", "host1", ["cpu4", "cpu5", "cpu6", "cpu7"]]`: Select only CPUs 4-7 of host1 in cluster1
3. `["cluster1", "host1"]`: Select the complete node. If querying for a CPU-specific metric such as flops, all CPUs are implied

## Config file

The config file is a JSON document with four top-level sections.

### `main`

```json
"main": {
  "addr": "0.0.0.0:8082",
  "https-cert-file": "",
  "https-key-file": "",
  "jwt-public-key": "<base64-encoded Ed25519 public key>",
  "user": "",
  "group": "",
  "backend-url": ""
}
```

- `addr`: Address and port to listen on (default: `0.0.0.0:8082`)
- `https-cert-file` / `https-key-file`: Paths to TLS certificate/key for HTTPS
- `jwt-public-key`: Base64-encoded Ed25519 public key for JWT authentication. If empty, no auth is required.
- `user` / `group`: Drop privileges to this user/group after startup
- `backend-url`: Optional URL of a cc-backend instance used as node provider

### `metrics`

Per-metric configuration. Each key is the metric name:

```json
"metrics": {
  "cpu_load": { "frequency": 60, "aggregation": null },
  "flops_any": { "frequency": 60, "aggregation": "sum" },
  "cpu_user":  { "frequency": 60, "aggregation": "avg" }
}
```

- `frequency`: Sampling interval in seconds
- `aggregation`: How to aggregate sub-level data: `"sum"`, `"avg"`, or `null` (no aggregation)

### `metric-store`

```json
"metric-store": {
  "checkpoints": {
    "interval": "12h",
    "directory": "./var/checkpoints"
  },
  "memory-cap": 100,
  "retention-in-memory": "48h",
  "cleanup": {
    "mode": "archive",
    "interval": "48h",
    "directory": "./var/archive"
  },
  "nats-subscriptions": [
    { "subscribe-to": "hpc-nats", "cluster-tag": "fritz" }
  ]
}
```

- `checkpoints.interval`: How often to write checkpoints to disk
- `checkpoints.directory`: Where to store checkpoint files
- `memory-cap`: Memory cap in MB
- `retention-in-memory`: How long to keep data in memory (e.g. `"48h"`)
- `cleanup.mode`: What to do with old data: `"archive"` or `"delete"`
- `cleanup.interval`: How often to run the cleanup
- `cleanup.directory`: Where to archive old data
- `nats-subscriptions`: List of NATS subjects to subscribe to, with associated cluster tag

### `nats`

```json
"nats": {
  "address": "nats://0.0.0.0:4222",
  "username": "root",
  "password": "root"
}
```

NATS connection is optional. If not configured, only the HTTP write endpoint is available.

For more information see the ClusterCockpit documentation [website](https://clustercockpit.org/docs/reference/cc-metric-store/ccms-configuration/).

## Test the complete setup (excluding cc-backend itself)

There are two ways for sending data to the cc-metric-store, both of which are
supported by the
[cc-metric-collector](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-collector).
This example uses NATS; the alternative is to use HTTP.

```sh
# Only needed once, downloads the docker image
docker pull nats:latest

# Start the NATS server
docker run -p 4222:4222 -ti nats:latest
```

Second, build and start the
[cc-metric-collector](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-collector)
using the following as Sink-Config:

```json
{
  "type": "nats",
  "host": "localhost",
  "port": "4222",
  "database": "updates"
}
```

Third, build and start the metric store. For this example here, the
`config.json` file already in the repository should work just fine.

```sh
# Assuming you have a clone of this repo in ./cc-metric-store:
cd cc-metric-store
make
./cc-metric-store
```

And finally, use the API to fetch some data. The API is protected by JWT based
authentication if `jwt-public-key` is set in `config.json`. You can use this JWT
for testing:
`REDACTED`

```sh
JWT="REDACTED"

# If the collector and store and nats-server have been running for at least 60 seconds on the same host:
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JWT" \
     "http://localhost:8082/api/query/" \
     -d '{
       "cluster": "testcluster",
       "from": '"$(expr $(date +%s) - 60)"',
       "to": '"$(date +%s)"',
       "queries": [{ "metric": "cpu_load", "host": "'"$(hostname)"'" }]
     }'
```

For debugging, the debug endpoint dumps the current content to stdout:

```sh
JWT="REDACTED"

# Dump everything
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JWT" "http://localhost:8082/api/debug/"

# Dump a specific selector (colon-separated path)
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $JWT" "http://localhost:8082/api/debug/?selector=testcluster:host1"
```

```json
  "group": "",

}
```

```json
  "cleanup": {

}
```

# ClusterCockpit Metric Store

[![Build & Test](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-store/actions/workflows/test.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-metric-store/actions/workflows/test.yml)

The cc-metric-store provides a simple in-memory time series database for storing
metrics of cluster nodes at preconfigured intervals. It is meant to be used as
part of the [ClusterCockpit suite](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit). As all
data is kept in-memory, accessing it is very fast. It also provides topology aware
aggregations over time _and_ nodes/sockets/cpus.

checkpoints (or via WAL on every write), not immediately as it is received.
Only the configured retention window is kept in memory.

The storage engine is provided by the
[cc-backend](https://github.com/ClusterCockpit/cc-backend) package
